    18|

Asset generation tools that produce final app assets support `runInBackground: true`; the 3D generation tools (`generate3DHumanoid` / `generate3DAsset`) always run in the background — preview, user approval, and generation included.    142|

   144|robots, anthropomorphic mascots) as USDZ + GLB, then rigs it and generates the selected
   145|skeletal animations. For props, vehicles, and non-humanoid creatures use generate3DAsset.
   146|
   147|Runs entirely in the background and immediately returns a generationId. The chat widget
   148|first shows a concept preview image (full body, A-pose); the user must explicitly approve
   149|or reject it directly on the widget (no decision within the approval window cancels the
   150|generation), and on approval the final Meshy generation continues automatically. After the mesh completes, the SAME widget shows
   151|an animation picker pre-seeded from the animations parameter (or an automatic
   152|recommendation); rigging + animations then run before the task resolves (accepted clips are
   153|bundled into Swift Resources/ on Swift workspaces; web workspaces reference the Rigged GLB +
   154|animation GLB URLs instead — no bundling).
   155|Announce the preview as "here is the character I'll build" — do not ask for permission in
   156|chat.
   157|
   158|waitTask returns either the finished character or the user's rejection feedback. If
   159|rejected, revise the prompt according to the feedback and call this tool again — the new
   160|call adds a fresh preview widget.
   161|
   162|The prompt must describe the character's body, clothing, and worn accessories only — no
   163|held weapons/props and no other characters (generate props separately with generate3DAsset).
   164|
   165|The finished model is saved to the user's Assets only after the whole flow completes.
   166|Generate different characters in parallel (separate calls), then waitTask every
   167|generationId in one call. Never call this tool twice for the same character unless the
   168|previous call returned rejected/failed.

   171|type Input = {
   172|    /** Short display name, e.g. 'Player Character', 'Wooden Crate'. Shown as the widget title and used as the bundled resource file name. */

   276|### How to generate image assets (backgrounds, illustrations, UI graphics)
   277|
   278|Use the `generateImageAsset` tool for image assets. Do NOT use `generateImage` for project assets. For app icons, read `.rork/skills/app-config/SKILL.md` and use `generateIcon`.
   279|
   280|Generate custom image assets using AI (OpenAI gpt-image-2 by default; transparent background assets use gpt-image-1.5). Returns image URLs hosted on R2.
   281|
   282|**Workflow:**
   283|
   284|1. Decide what images the app needs (backgrounds, illustrations, banners, etc.)
   285|2. If you need to check for duplicates or reuse an existing asset, read `.rork/skills/asset-library/SKILL.md` and call `listExistingAssets` with the matching `types` filter
   286|3. Call `generateImageAsset` via `callTool` with `runInBackground: true` for each image
   287|4. Continue building the app while generation runs — store each returned `generationId`
   288|5. At the end, call `waitTask({ generationIds: [...] })` once with every pending `generationId`, then use the returned URLs in code
   289|
   290|
   291|**Sizes:** `"1024x1024"` (square, default), `"1024x1536"` (portrait), `"1536x1024"` (landscape)
   292|
   293|**Background:** Use `"transparent"` whenever the user asks for transparent/no background, cutouts, alpha PNGs, sprites, stickers, or UI assets without a backdrop. Use `"opaque"` for photos/scenes or solid backgrounds. Use `"auto"` only when the background is unspecified.
   294|
   295|**Edit mode:** Pass `inputImages` array with URLs to edit/composite existing images
   296|
   297|**Tips for prompts:**
   298|
   299|- Be specific about style, colors, composition, and where it will be used
   300|- For app backgrounds: describe the mood, gradient, pattern
   301|- For illustrations: describe the subject, style (flat, 3D, watercolor), colors
   302|- For transparent assets: set `background: "transparent"` in the tool call, not only in the prompt
   303|- Use `assetName` as a short snake_case identifier: `hero_banner`, `onboarding_bg`, `empty_state_illustration`
   304|
   305|
   306|**NEVER retry failed image generations.** If an image fails, the user sees the error on the card and can retry manually.
   307|
   308|### How to generate audio (music, sound effects, voice, and dialogue)
   309|
   310|Use the `generateAudio` tool for audio assets, including voiceovers and narration.
   311|
   312|Generate background music, sound effects, voice, and dialogue using ElevenLabs AI. Returns audio URLs (MP3) hosted on R2, accessible via `https://rork.app/pa/{projectId}/{uniqueKey}`.
   313|
   314|**Workflow:**
   315|
   316|1. Decide what audio the app needs (background music, UI sounds, ambient, etc.)
   317|2. Call `generateAudio` via `callTool` with `runInBackground: true` for each audio asset
   318|3. Continue building the app while generation runs — store each returned `generationId`
   319|4. At the end, call `waitTask({ generationIds: [...] })` once with every pending `generationId`, then use the returned `uniqueKey` URLs
   320|
   321|
   322|**Types:**
   323|
   324|- `"music"` — background tracks, soundtracks (3s to 10min). Use `forceInstrumental: true` for no vocals. Use `musicLengthMs` to control duration.
   325|- `"sound_effect"` — short SFX up to 30s (clicks, explosions, ambience). Use `durationSeconds` to control length, `loop: true` for seamless loops.
   326|- `"voice"` — text-to-speech with a specific voice (NPC lines, narration, instructions). Requires `voiceId` — call `listVoices` first. Supports emotion markers: `[cheerfully]`, `[whispering]`, `[angry]`, `[stuttering]`.
   327|- `"dialogue"` — multi-voice conversations (cutscenes, NPC dialogues). Pass `dialogueInputs` array with `{ text, voiceId }` pairs. Up to 10 unique voices. Supports emotion markers in text.
   328|
   329|
   330|**Tips for prompts:**
   331|
   332|- Music: describe genre, mood, instruments, tempo. "Upbeat chiptune 8-bit adventure theme, 120 BPM, loopable"
   333|- SFX: be specific. "Wooden door creaking open slowly, with rusty hinges" not just "door sound"
   334|- Voice: write the exact text to speak. Add emotion markers: "[excited] Welcome to the arena, brave warrior!"
   335|- Dialogue: use descriptive text per character. "[whispering] The treasure is behind the waterfall."
   336|- Use `assetName` as a short snake_case identifier: `battle_theme`, `door_open`, `npc_greeting`
   337|
   338|
   339|**Voice selection:** Call `listVoices` to see available voices with IDs, names, and labels (gender, accent, age). Pick voices that match your characters.
   340|
   341|**In code:** Reference audio via `https://rork.app/pa/{projectId}/{uniqueKey}`. Download the MP3 to local cache on first use, then play locally for zero latency. See the "Play Audio from URL" recipe in `audio-patterns.md` for the complete pattern.
   342|
   343|### How to generate video assets
   344|
   345|Use the `generateVideo` tool for video assets.
   346|
   347|Generate video clips using AI (Vercel AI Gateway). Returns video URLs (MP4) hosted on R2.
   348|
   349|**Flow:**
   350|
   351|1. Decide what videos the app needs
   352|2. Call `generateVideo` via `callTool` with `runInBackground: true` for each video
   353|3. Continue building the app while generation runs — store each returned `generationId`
   354|4. At the end, call `waitTask({ generationIds: [...] })` once with every pending `generationId`, then use the returned video URLs/`uniqueKey`s
   355|
   356|
   357|**Video types (5 modes):**
   358|
   359|- `text-to-video` (default): Generate from a text prompt. Models: Google Veo, KlingAI, Seedance, Wan, Grok.
   360|- `image-to-video`: Animate a static image into a video. Provide `inputImageUrl`.
   361|- `reference-to-video`: Generate a new scene featuring characters from reference images. Provide `referenceUrls` (1-5 URLs). Use `character1`, `character2` in prompt to reference each.
   362|- `motion-control`: Transfer motion from a reference video onto a character image. Provide `inputImageUrl` (character) + `motionVideoUrl` (reference motion).
   363|- `video-editing`: Edit an existing video with text prompts. Provide `editVideoUrl`. Describe the changes in `prompt`.
   364|
   365|
   366|**Parameters:**
   367|
   368|- `duration`: Depends on model. Veo: 4/6/8s. KlingAI: 5/10s. Seedance: 4-12s. Wan: 2-15s. Grok: 1-15s. Default 8.
   369|- `aspectRatio`: "16:9" (default) or "9:16".
   370|
   371|
   372|**Generation takes 1-5 minutes per video.** Always use `runInBackground: true` and generate in parallel.
   373|
   374|**NEVER retry failed video generations.** If a video fails, the user sees the error on the card and can retry manually. Do NOT call `generateVideo` again for a video that already failed.
   375|
   376|### How to generate 3D models
   377|
   378|Two tools cover every 3D generation, one call per model:
   379|
   380|- `generate3DHumanoid` — human-shaped characters that should be animatable (humans, knights, zombies, robots, mascots with one head / two arms / two legs). Handles preview approval, Meshy generation, rigging, and animation selection on ONE widget. Pick animation actionIds from `animation-library.md` in this skill and pass them as `animations` to pre-seed the picker.
   381|- `generate3DAsset` — everything else: props, vehicles, weapons, buildings, food, furniture, and non-humanoid creatures.
   382|
   383|
   384|Both tools run entirely in the background and immediately return a `generationId` — like every other generation tool. The chat widget first shows a concept preview image that the user must explicitly approve or reject directly on the widget (no decision within the approval window cancels the generation); on approval the final Meshy generation continues automatically. Announce the preview as "here is the model I'll build", never ask for permission before calling the tool. `waitTask` returns either the finished model or `{ action: "rejected", feedback }` — in that case revise the prompt per the feedback and call the SAME tool again (a fresh preview widget appears).
   385|
   386|For humanoid characters, keep the prompt rigging-safe: body, clothing, colors, hair, facial expression, and wearable accessories are fine, but do not add books, tools, weapons, sports balls, instruments, bags, foot-mounted gear, nearby props, or anything held/carried/attached unless the user explicitly asked for that standalone prop as a separate model (generate it with `generate3DAsset`). Hands, arms, legs, and feet must stay clearly visible.
   387|
   388|Completed models return USDZ and thumbnail URLs hosted on R2 and are saved to the user's Assets only after the whole generation (including the humanoid animation phase) finishes successfully.
   389|
   390|**When to use:** Whenever the app needs characters, creatures, vehicles, props, or any object that would look significantly better as a real 3D model than as procedural geometry.
   391|
   392|**When NOT to use — use procedural geometry instead:**
   393|
   394|- Floors, walls, platforms, ground planes → `generatePlane` / `generateBox`
   395|- Bullets, projectiles, particles → `generateSphere` / `generateCylinder`
   396|- Simple flat coins or tokens → `generateCylinder` (flat) with a material. Only use `collectible` role for detailed/3D pickups like treasure chests, ornate gems, trophies.
   397|- Simple barriers, fences, pillars → `generateBox` / `generateCylinder`
   398|- Any shape that is essentially a box, sphere, cylinder, or cone
   399|
   400|
   401|**Scene composition — keep scenes lightweight:**
   402|
   403|- Limit to **~8 unique generated models** per scene. More variety = worse performance.
   404|- If you need **multiple copies** of the same object (5 trees, 10 coins, 20 asteroids), generate ONE model and use `entity.clone(recursive: true)` or `MeshInstancesComponent` (see `game-performance-reference.md`). Do NOT generate separate models for each copy.
   405|- For games with many on-screen objects: generate 3–5 hero models + use procedural geometry for everything else.
   406|- iPhone-oriented rule of thumb: **keep the rendered scene around the ~200K triangle budget** counting every clone, not just unique models.
   407|
   408|
   409|**Performance:** A preview image takes ~10-30 seconds. Each Meshy model takes ~2 minutes and costs credits. The generation tools never block — preview, approval, and generation all run in the background. Build the entire app first, then collect results with one `waitTask({ generationIds: [...] })` at the end.
   410|
   411|**Failed generations:** if `waitTask` reports a failed 3D generation, you may re-schedule that ONE model with a new tool call and call `waitTask` again with the still-pending ids plus the new generationId. If it fails twice, move on and use procedural geometry as a fallback.
   412|
   413|#### Polygon budget — `polycount`
   414|
   415|The scene has a **global triangle budget of ~200K** for generated models. Set `polycount` per call from the model's role and divide by the number of planned clones (clones render their full triangles each — `entity.clone` and `MeshInstancesComponent` save draw calls, NOT triangles):
   416|
   417|| Model's role in the scene                          | polycount for a single instance |
   418|| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
   419|| Hero / main character                              | 30 000 – 60 000                 |
   420|| Secondary characters, creatures, vehicles          | 10 000 – 25 000                 |
   421|| Detailed props (weapons, furniture, tools)         | 5 000 – 10 000                  |
   422|| Simple props / collectibles (crates, balls, gems)  | 2 000 – 5 000                   |
   423|| Background decoration (trees, rocks, distant fill) | 1 000 – 3 000                   |
   424|
   425|**Example** — a game with 5 unique models, trees and rocks cloned:
   426|
   427|```
   428|generate3DHumanoid({ name: "Knight", prompt: "...", animations: [30, 19, 2], polycount: 45000 })  // hero
   429|generate3DHumanoid({ name: "Goblin", prompt: "...", polycount: 8000 })                            // 3 clones → 24K rendered
   430|generate3DAsset({ name: "Sword", prompt: "...", polycount: 8000 })
   431|generate3DAsset({ name: "Tree", prompt: "...", polycount: 2000 })                                 // 8 clones → 16K rendered
   432|generate3DAsset({ name: "Rock", prompt: "...", polycount: 1500 })                                 // 4 clones → 6K rendered
   433|// Rendered scene total: 45K + 24K + 8K + 16K + 6K ≈ 99K — well inside the 200K budget.
   434|// Still generate ONE model per asset kind and use entity.clone(recursive: true) for copies.
   435|
   436|```
   437|
   438|Omit `polycount` to use the default (15 000) — fine for a single mid-importance model, wasteful for cloned decorations.
   439|
   440|#### Workflow
   441|
   442|Choose Swift/RealityKit generated-3D game orientation before `createApp`. Orientation follows the requested gameplay, camera, and controls, not the fact that the scene is 3D. Use `swiftTemplateProfile: "landscape-3d-game"` when the chosen orientation is landscape: explicit landscape/horizontal requests, generic or ambiguous Swift/RealityKit 3D games, racing/driving/flight, FPS/TPS/shooters, arena combat, action RPG/open-world/adventure, sports/fighting, dual-stick controls, wide tactical maps, or designs that need a wide camera view or two-thumb controls. Keep portrait/default orientation for portrait-first games: endless/lane runners, vertical runners/climbers/fallers, one-tap or swipe hypercasual games, idle/merge/tycoon/collection games, card/board/puzzle/gacha games, vertical shooters, rhythm lane games, regular apps, AR/model viewers, product viewers, and non-game 3D showcases. If a generic 3D game is ambiguous, do not ask; default to landscape and use `swiftTemplateProfile: "landscape-3d-game"`. For an existing Swift app whose chosen design is landscape, set the main iOS target's iPhone/iPad supported orientations to LandscapeLeft/LandscapeRight, set `INFOPLIST_KEY_UIRequiresFullScreen` to `YES`, and mark the app's `rork.json` settings with `previewOrientation: "landscape"` before final RealityKit integration. Keep device orientation separate from generated-model local axes.
   443|
   444|1. Read the app's code to understand the design, style, and context
   445|2. Decide which objects need 3D models, pick a `polycount` for each, and split them into humanoids (animatable characters) vs assets
   446|3. For humanoids, read `animation-library.md` in this skill first and pick the actionIds the app's code will drive (idle, walk, run, jump, attack, ...)
   447|4. Before the generation calls, write ONE short sentence acknowledging what you're about to generate (e.g. "Here are the models I'll build — preview cards are coming up; reject a card to change it before generation"). Then call `generate3DHumanoid` / `generate3DAsset` once per model. Each call immediately returns a `generationId` while the preview, the user's approval, and the final generation all run in the background. In prompts, describe visible semantic landmarks, never local coordinate axes: say "muzzle clearly visible at one end, stock at the opposite end" or "windshield and grille visible at the front", not "+X", "-Z", "positive X axis", "negative Z direction", or "model local axis".
   448|5. If `waitTask` returns `{ action: "rejected", feedback }` for a model, revise the prompt per the feedback and call the same tool again (a fresh preview widget appears). If it returns `cancelled`, drop that model and ask what the user wants instead.
   449|6. **Split asset-independent code from final generated-model placement code.** Do NOT write final RealityKit transforms, socket constants, camera framing, model scale/orientation constants, or assembly offsets from preview thumbnails or pre-bundle guesses. For Swift/RealityKit generated-3D games, the canonical sequence is `generate3DHumanoid` / `generate3DAsset` (preview approval happens on the widget while the task runs) → asset-independent code only → `waitTask({ generationIds: [...] })` → `add3DModelToProject` for static models (humanoids with accepted animations are bundled automatically) → final scene code → `runChecks`. While Meshy generation runs, write state, input, HUD, audio hooks, scoring, systems, resource-name constants, and procedural fallbacks. Final placement code uses the ORIENTATION METADATA reported by the generation and bundling tool outputs.
   450|7. **Before writing final generated-model RealityKit placement/assembly code**, call `waitTask` once with every 3D generationId. If a generation failed, you may re-schedule that one model and re-wait; if it fails again, keep the procedural fallback. The humanoid result already covers the animation phase (the user confirmed the selection on the widget while generation ran) and reports the bundled model + animation resourceNames. For static models call `add3DModelToProject`.
   451|8. Once a generation completes, follow the tool output:
   452|  - For Swift apps where the model must be referenced by name in code (`Entity(named:)` / `Model3D(named:)`): humanoids with accepted animations are bundled automatically by the tool; bundle static models with `add3DModelToProject`. Each tool output reports the bundled `resourceName` and the model's persisted ORIENTATION METADATA — write the placement code yourself per the recipe in `.rork/skills/swift/game-asset-integration.md` (gameplay container → runtime child → normalized visual; body-frame correction from `localFrontAxis` + `localUpAxis`). For composed objects (vehicle + wheels, weapon body + magazine + bolt, humanoid + hands), create one assembly root with named socket children, measure socket offsets from the corrected visual bounds after bundling, and keep part motion axes (wheel spin, slide recoil) separate from `localFrontAxis`.
   453|  - For ambient asset-first generations (the user just asked for a 3D thing without integration intent): leave the model in the user's Assets and ask in chat before integrating.
   454|  - For web (Vite React) workspaces: reference each model's GLB URL with three.js / React Three Fiber per `web/three-js.md` in this skill — there is no bundling step on web; the orientation metadata arrives in each generation tool's output (and via `listExistingAssets` for reused models). For other non-Swift workspaces: reference the USDZ/GLB URL through the active platform's loader.
   455|  - Use the returned `orientation` metadata for every `Entity(named:)` / `Model3D(named:)` load, including RealityKit scenes, `ARView`, `UIViewRepresentable`, and SwiftUI overlay/composited game pieces. If `hasIntrinsicFront=true`, choose `desiredWorldForward` from the object's gameplay role, not from the camera, preview, or thumbnail: player/NPC uses movement or facing direction, vehicle uses travel direction, obstacle/sign/door meant to face the player uses the vector toward the player or camera target. Do NOT use raw `.pi` / `.pi / 2` constants in a `rotation(for:)` helper for generated models. If adding wobble, lean, or animation, apply it to the runtime child or compose deltas from a saved rest transform instead of overwriting the normalized visual/base socket transform. Use a gameplay container, a runtime child, and a loaded visual child; normalize scale by the semantic size being set (height for characters/floor props, front-axis length for long weapon/vehicle/bolt parts, longest axis only for directionless dominant-extent sizing), center X/Z, and ground/center/top-anchor Y from post-transform `visualBounds(relativeTo: runtimeChild)`. Move the gameplay container to the world/anchor position instead. Do not only fix `bounds.min.y`. If `hasIntrinsicFront=false`, treat the model as directionless for yaw correction, but still define explicit socket names and motion axes when it is a part of an assembly. For forward-scrolling endless runners where the camera follows from `+Z` and the track extends toward `-Z`, runner `desiredWorldForward` is `[0, 0, -1]`; an oncoming tram facing the runner uses `[0, 0, 1]`.
   456|9. **Humanoid animations.** `generate3DHumanoid` handles the animation flow on its own widget: after the mesh completes it shows an animation picker (pre-seeded from the `animations` parameter or an automatic recommendation), waits for the user's confirmation, then rigs the model, generates the selected clips, and bundles them into Swift Resources/. The `waitTask` result reports the exact bundled `resourceName` strings plus an `animationPhase` outcome:
   457|Do not invent animation `actionId`s in code or in chat — every actionId must come from `animation-library.md` or existing animations via `listExistingAssets`.
   458|  - **Animations succeeded** — wire the reported resource names into Swift using the pattern in `.rork/skills/swift/generated-3d-animations.md` (write the small animation-player type from that doc yourself): one player per generated model container, exact resource names preloaded once, loops and one-shots driven from gameplay state. For states without listed resources, stop the stale loop so walk/run playback stops and the base visual is restored. Do not guess resource names. Read `.rork/skills/swift/realitykit-meshy-animation.md` for the bundled-filename contract and Meshy pitfalls.
   459|  - **Web (Vite React) workspaces** — there is no bundling: after `waitTask` resolves, call `listExistingAssets({ types: ["3d-model"] })` for the model's Rigged GLB URL and each accepted animation's GLB URL, then wire playback per `web/three-js.md`: render the Rigged GLB as the visual (the plain model GLB has no skeleton — clips bound to it silently do nothing) and play the clips on one `AnimationMixer` bound to that rigged scene.
   460|  - **Animations skipped or not rig-ready** — the result says why (user skipped, or the mesh failed the rigging readiness check: `not_full_body` → regenerate with "full body, head to feet, no cropping"; `limbs_unclear` → "clear arms and legs visible, no occluding clothing"; `crossed_limbs` → A-pose "standing, arms held slightly out, legs apart"; `occluded_limbs` → no props or capes hiding joints). Treat the model as static, surface the reason briefly, and offer to regenerate with a clearer body plan when rigging failed.
   461|  - **Follow-up animation requests** ("also add a dance") — pick actionIds from `animation-library.md` in this skill, then call `append3DAnimations({ modelId, animations })` and `waitTask`. Patch app code before claiming the action works only when the user asked for gameplay behavior, controls, or playback wiring. A bundled animation USDZ is not gameplay by itself: add or update input, state/timers, hitbox or movement effects when relevant, and the animation-player state mapping before claiming a mechanic such as slide, attack, dodge, jump, or wave works.
   462|  - To drop an animation the user no longer wants later, call `remove3DModelAnimation` (idempotent — succeeds with `alreadyAbsent: true` if it's already gone; throws CONFLICT while the model is currently being animated, in which case wait for the in-progress animation to finish and retry).
   463|  - To enumerate animations a user has already added through the in-app Animate panel, call `listExistingAssets({ types: ["3d-model"] })` — animation row ids and names are surfaced under each model.
   464|
   465|
   466|### Image-to-3D generation
   467|
   468|Both generation tools accept reference images via `imageUrls`; the array length decides the flow:
   469|
   470|- **One image** — image-to-3D with the normal preview-approval step: `generate3DAsset({ name: "Toy", prompt: "Create a 3D model from this reference image", imageUrls: ["https://..."] })`. The preview widget shows what will be built; the user approves or rejects with feedback as usual.
   471|- **2-4 images of the SAME object from different angles** — direct Meshy multi-image-to-3D, skipping the preview (the user's own photos are the approval surface). Only use this when the user explicitly provides multiple reference images — never generate images to use as multi-image input. `lowPoly` is not supported with multi-image references.
   472|
   473|
   474|Rules:
   475|
   476|- Image must be a public https URL (user attachments and generated outputs are public)
   477|- Supported formats: jpg, jpeg, png
   478|- NEVER invent or guess URLs
   479|- Multi-image direct pipeline takes ~2 minutes; single-image requests create a preview first
   480|
   481|
   482|**Editing an existing model** — there are no retexture/remesh tools; edits are fresh generations. When the user attaches a generated asset (or names one) and asks for changes — different texture, colors, lower poly count, geometry tweaks — call the same generation tool again: pass the attached image (or the model's thumbnail URL from `listExistingAssets`) in `imageUrls` and fold the requested changes into the prompt (e.g. "same knight as the reference image, but with golden armor"). Use `lowPoly`/`polycount` for density changes. The previous model stays in the user's Assets until they delete it.
   483|
   484|Tips for good prompts:
   485|
   486|- Describe a **single object** — multi-object prompts produce poor results
   487|- Generate EXACTLY what was requested. A ball is a sphere, a tree is a tree. Do NOT add humanoid features (limbs, faces, arms, legs) to non-humanoid objects.
   488|- Front-load important descriptors: "low-poly cartoon red dragon with wings spread" not "a dragon that is red and low-poly"
   489|- Mention style when it matters, but do not add cartoon/stylized unless the user or project explicitly calls for it; otherwise prefer realistic or clean 3D preview wording.
   490|- Include physical details: colors, materials, proportions
   491|- Never describe a pose in the prompt — the API handles posing automatically.
   492|- For game props: mention scale context ("hand-held sword", "chest-high barrel")
   493|- Bad: "a dragon" — Good: "a low-poly cartoon red dragon with wings spread, facing forward"
   494|- Bad: "a soccer ball with arms and legs" — Good: "a realistic soccer ball with black and white hexagonal panels"
   495|
   496|
   497|**In code:** See `realitykit-reference.md` for the `downloadModel` + `RealityView` pattern to load generated USDZ files. Always prefer generated models over procedural geometry for characters, creatures, vehicles, and detailed props.

(End of file - total 497 lines)

I'm starting with the app icon and property images, then moving into building out the IVX Holdings app—a premium real estate investment platform with an elegant dark theme using deep navy and charcoal paired with gold and champagne accents. For the icon, I need a centered gold geometric building or tower symbol on a deep navy background, keeping it abstract and monogram-like without any text.

Next I'm generating three landscape property hero images showcasing a luxury residential development, a commercial mixed-use project, and waterfront villas. Since Swift automatically bundles image assets into Assets.xcassets, I can reference them directly using UIImage(named:) with the asset names the tool provides. I'll structure the app with a TabView for Deals, How It Works, Portfolio, and Profile sections, then build out the supporting files—a theme utility for colors and gradients, Deal and AppState models, and individual views for the deals list, detail cards, intake flow, and saved deals. I'll keep concurrency at the default setting. I'll use those asset names for the deal images and now move into building out the core files. Setting up a Theme enum with the color palette—deep navy background, champagne gold accents, and semantic colors for status indicators. The Deal model will be a nonisolated Codable struct to keep it thread-safe, and AppState will handle membership tracking, email verification, and allocation requests with UserDefaults persistence. Starting to build the view hierarchy with a TabView structure. Generating design assets...I'm scheduling the app icon and three property hero images to load in the background while I build the app.
